_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-referenced against NeuPSIG 2015 grading algorithm (Finnerup Lancet Neurol), IASP 2017 neuropathic pain definition, NICE NG193 (2019), AAN 2022 painful diabetic neuropathy guideline, FDA labels for gabapentin/pregabalin/duloxetine/lidocaine 5% patch/capsaicin 8% patch (QUTENZA), and SENZA-RCT for 10 kHz spinal cord stimulation.

h1Title: Neuropathic Pain: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Neuropathic pain is chronic pain caused by damage or disease of the somatosensory nervous system itself, rather than ongoing tissue injury. It affects an estimated 7-10% of the general population, with diabetic peripheral neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia, sciatica, post-stroke central pain, and chemotherapy-induced neuropathy accounting for most cases. The pain is typically described as burning, electric shock-like, shooting, or stabbing, often with numbness, tingling, and exquisite sensitivity to light touch on the same skin area. Standard analgesics including NSAIDs and opioids work poorly; first-line treatment relies on gabapentinoids, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, tricyclic antidepressants, and targeted topical agents. Untreated neuropathic pain drives sleep loss, depression, disability, and a roughly 30% reduction in health-related quality of life on validated measures.

keyStats.prevalence: Roughly 7-10% of adults globally have neuropathic pain (van Hecke 2014 meta-analysis); 6.9% in a French general-population survey (Bouhassira 2008)
keyStats.demographics: Women slightly more affected than men; prevalence rises with age and with diabetes duration
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in adults 50-70; postherpetic neuralgia mainly over age 60; diabetic neuropathy begins 5-15 years after diabetes diagnosis
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 400-700 million people worldwide have a neuropathic pain component to their chronic pain (IASP 2017 working estimates)

definition: Neuropathic pain (ICD-10: G89.4 chronic pain syndrome; G62.9 polyneuropathy, unspecified; G63 polyneuropathy in diseases classified elsewhere) is defined by the International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP, 2017) as pain caused by a lesion or disease of the somatosensory nervous system. The lesion can sit anywhere along the pain pathway — peripheral nerve, dorsal root ganglion, spinal cord, brainstem, thalamus, or cortex — and produces aberrant signalling rather than appropriate detection of tissue damage. Pathophysiology involves peripheral sensitisation through ectopic firing of injured axons and dorsal root ganglion neurons, upregulation of sodium channels Nav1.7 and Nav1.8, microglial activation in the dorsal horn, central sensitisation via NMDA-receptor potentiation, and loss of descending inhibitory control from brainstem nuclei. The 2015 NeuPSIG grading algorithm (Finnerup, Lancet Neurology) classifies a patient as having possible, probable, or definite neuropathic pain based on pain distribution in a plausible neuroanatomical territory, a relevant lesion on history, sensory signs on examination, and confirmatory testing. The condition is managed by pain medicine specialists, neurologists, and primary care, with rehabilitation, mental health, and palliative care input as needed.

typesClassification.description: The most common cause worldwide. Length-dependent symmetric polyneuropathy beginning in the toes, with burning, tingling, and allodynia. Affects roughly 20% of people with diabetes for ten years or more.
Persistent dermatomal pain lasting more than 3 months after a shingles rash. Risk rises sharply with age — about 10-15% of patients over 60 with shingles develop PHN.
Nerve root compression or inflammation, usually from disc herniation or foraminal stenosis. Pain follows a dermatomal pattern down the leg or arm.
Distal symmetric neuropathy from platinum agents, taxanes, vinca alkaloids, bortezomib, and thalidomide. Symptoms can persist for years after treatment ends.
Brief, electric-shock-like facial pain in the distribution of one or more trigeminal divisions, typically triggered by light touch, chewing, or cold air.
Burning or aching pain in the body region affected by stroke, usually beginning weeks to months after the event. Affects 8-10% of stroke survivors.
Below-level neuropathic pain after traumatic or inflammatory spinal cord injury; often described as burning or band-like.
Pain perceived in a missing limb, occurring in 50-80% of amputees. Mechanisms involve peripheral nerve injury, central reorganisation, and altered body schema.

primarySymptoms: Constant or near-constant burning pain in the affected territory, often worst at night and interfering with sleep — described by patients as 'on fire' or 'sunburned from the inside'.
Brief, paroxysmal electric-shock-like or shooting pains lasting seconds, occurring spontaneously or triggered by minor stimuli such as touch or movement.
Allodynia — pain provoked by stimuli that should not be painful, such as the brush of clothing, a bedsheet, or a light breeze across the skin.
Hyperalgesia — disproportionately severe pain to mildly painful stimuli, such as a pinprick feeling like a deep stab.
Numbness, hypoesthesia, or 'wooden' sensation coexisting with the pain in the same area, with the paradoxical pattern of feeling both numb and intensely painful at once.
Tingling, pins-and-needles, or crawling sensations (paresthesia) and unpleasant abnormal sensations (dysesthesia) such as ants under the skin.
Symmetric distal distribution in length-dependent neuropathies — beginning in the toes and progressing upward in a stocking pattern, later involving the fingertips in a glove pattern.
Dermatomal or single-nerve distribution in radiculopathies and mononeuropathies, with pain confined to one nerve's territory rather than scattered.
Autonomic signs in the affected skin including colour change, dry or sweaty skin, hair loss, and temperature asymmetry, especially in complex regional pain syndrome and small-fibre neuropathy.
Sleep fragmentation, fatigue, low mood, and impaired concentration — common secondary symptoms that are now considered part of the neuropathic pain syndrome, not separate conditions.
earlyWarningSigns: Intermittent tingling or pins-and-needles in both feet that worsens at night, especially in someone with diabetes, prediabetes, or chronic alcohol use
A patch of altered sensation following a previous shingles rash that has not fully settled after 8-12 weeks
Shooting pain down one leg or arm following a back injury, lift, or prolonged sitting
New burning or numbness in the hands and feet during or after chemotherapy with a platinum, taxane, or vinca agent
Persistent facial pain triggered by chewing, tooth brushing, or cold air that has been mistakenly treated as dental pain
emergencySigns: Sudden weakness, foot drop, loss of bladder or bowel control, or saddle anaesthesia — possible cauda equina syndrome requiring urgent MRI and surgical decompression
Rapidly ascending numbness and weakness over hours to days — possible Guillain-Barré syndrome or transverse myelitis, both medical emergencies
Pain plus a spreading red rash with fever — possible necrotising soft-tissue infection or zoster sine herpete
New severe headache plus facial weakness, slurred speech, or visual loss with limb pain — possible stroke or central nervous system event
Severe burning pain with skin colour change, swelling, and stiffness after a recent fracture or surgery — possible early complex regional pain syndrome, where early treatment changes outcome

causes.description: Chronic hyperglycaemia damages small unmyelinated and large myelinated fibres through polyol pathway flux, advanced glycation end products, oxidative stress, and microvascular ischaemia of the vasa nervorum. Accounts for the single largest share of neuropathic pain globally.

Varicella-zoster virus reactivates from dorsal root ganglia in adults with waning cell-mediated immunity, producing dermatomal rash and direct neuronal injury that can persist as postherpetic neuralgia in 10-15% of patients over 60.

Disc herniation, foraminal stenosis, and spondylolisthesis compress lumbar or cervical nerve roots, producing radicular pain and sciatica. Inflammation around the root, not just mechanical pressure, drives much of the pain.

Platinum compounds, taxanes, vinca alkaloids, bortezomib, thalidomide, isoniazid, metronidazole, and antiretrovirals damage peripheral axons or dorsal root ganglion neurons. Risk is dose-dependent and may be permanent.

causes.cause: Stroke, multiple sclerosis, and spinal cord lesions
causes.description: Lesions in central somatosensory pathways — thalamus, brainstem, spinothalamic tract — produce central neuropathic pain. About 8-10% of stroke survivors develop central post-stroke pain, often months after the initial event.

Direct nerve transection, stretch, or entrapment during surgery (hernia repair, mastectomy, thoracotomy, amputation) produces chronic post-surgical neuropathic pain in 5-30% of patients depending on procedure.

Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy, Sjögren syndrome, sarcoidosis, vasculitis, and HIV-related neuropathy damage peripheral nerves through immune mechanisms and produce neuropathic pain.

causes.cause: Alcohol use disorder and nutritional deficiency
causes.description: Chronic heavy alcohol use and deficiencies of vitamin B12, B1 (thiamine), B6, and copper produce length-dependent polyneuropathy. Pain is common in the alcohol-related and B12-deficient subtypes.

riskFactors.factor: Diabetes duration and poor glycaemic control
riskFactors.description: Risk of painful neuropathy rises by roughly 2% per year of diabetes and accelerates with HbA1c above 8%. Tight glycaemic control reduces incidence in type 1 diabetes but has a smaller effect in type 2.
Postherpetic neuralgia incidence after shingles rises from under 5% under age 50 to roughly 20% over 80. Nerve regeneration capacity falls with age.
Recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) prevents over 90% of shingles cases and roughly 88% of postherpetic neuralgia in adults over 50 (ZOE-50 and ZOE-70 trials).
Cumulative dose of oxaliplatin, cisplatin, paclitaxel, vincristine, and bortezomib drives risk. Dose modification or substitution can reduce incidence in some regimens.
Consumption above 35 standard drinks per week is associated with clinically significant polyneuropathy. Risk is partially reversible with abstinence and thiamine repletion.

Affects roughly 20% of adults over 60 and is over-represented in patients on long-term metformin or proton-pump inhibitors. Replacement reverses early neuropathy but established axonal damage often persists.

Women have modestly higher prevalence of neuropathic pain across most causes, partly explained by higher rates of fibromyalgia, autoimmune disease, and chemotherapy exposure (breast and gynaecologic cancers).

riskFactors.factor: Genetic variants in sodium-channel genes (SCN9A, SCN10A, SCN11A)
riskFactors.description: Gain-of-function mutations in Nav1.7, Nav1.8, and Nav1.9 cause inherited erythromelalgia, paroxysmal extreme pain disorder, and small-fibre neuropathy. Polymorphisms also modify pain severity in common neuropathies.
Disc degeneration, spondylolisthesis, and central or foraminal stenosis underlie most adult radiculopathy. Heavy lifting and smoking accelerate disc degeneration.
Both untreated HIV and older nucleoside analogues (didanosine, stavudine) cause painful neuropathy. Modern regimens have lower neurotoxicity but legacy exposure persists.

affectedDemographics: General population prevalence: 6.9% in France (Bouhassira 2008), 8.2% in the UK (Torrance 2006), and an estimated 7-10% across Europe and North America
Among adults with type 2 diabetes for 10 or more years, painful diabetic neuropathy affects roughly 20-25%
Postherpetic neuralgia affects 10-15% of patients over 60 who develop shingles; over age 80, the rate approaches 20%
Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy affects 30-40% of patients during treatment with neurotoxic regimens, with 30% having persistent symptoms at 6 months and a smaller share at 2 years
Central post-stroke pain develops in 8-10% of stroke survivors, with thalamic strokes carrying the highest risk
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of neuropathic pain is primarily clinical, anchored on the 2015 NeuPSIG grading algorithm (Finnerup, Lancet Neurology). The clinician first establishes whether the pain has a plausible neuroanatomical distribution — does the territory correspond to a peripheral nerve, dermatome, plexus, spinal cord level, or central pathway. Step two is a history of a relevant lesion or disease that can explain the distribution, such as long-standing diabetes, a previous shingles rash, a known disc herniation, a stroke, or recent neurotoxic chemotherapy. Step three is a focused sensory examination — testing for hypoesthesia and hyperalgesia with pinprick, allodynia with a soft brush, thermal hypoesthesia with warm and cool objects, and vibration loss with a 128 Hz tuning fork. Reflex changes and motor signs are documented when present. Confirmatory testing depends on the suspected lesion. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography document large-fibre neuropathy and localise mononeuropathies and radiculopathies but are normal in pure small-fibre disease. Skin biopsy for intraepidermal nerve fibre density confirms small-fibre neuropathy. MRI defines structural lesions of brain, spinal cord, and roots. Quantitative sensory testing has a research role and helps phenotype patients for trial enrolment. Validated screening tools — the painDETECT, DN4, and LANSS questionnaires — distinguish neuropathic from nociceptive pain in primary care with sensitivities of 80-85%. Three certainty levels emerge from this workflow: possible (criteria 1-2 met), probable (criteria 1-3 met), and definite (criteria 1-4 met, with confirmatory testing). Most treatment guidelines apply once probable status is reached.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the pattern of sensory loss and gain — hypoesthesia, allodynia, hyperalgesia, thermal and vibration thresholds — and maps the distribution against known neuroanatomy. The cornerstone of the NeuPSIG algorithm.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute examination using a pinprick, a soft brush or cotton wisp, a tuning fork, and warm and cool objects. The clinician asks you to compare sides and report changes; nothing is invasive and there is no pain risk beyond the symptom itself.

diagnosticTests.test: Validated screening questionnaire (DN4, painDETECT, or LANSS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes neuropathic from nociceptive pain with 80-85% sensitivity. Used in primary care to triage who needs further work-up and in clinical trials for phenotyping.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short paper or digital form completed in 5 minutes asking about burning, tingling, electric shocks, and sensitivity to touch.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms large-fibre peripheral neuropathy, localises mononeuropathies (carpal tunnel, ulnar at elbow, peroneal at fibular head), and documents radiculopathy. Normal in pure small-fibre neuropathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrodes are placed on the skin and brief electrical pulses stimulate the nerve. EMG involves a thin needle electrode in selected muscles. The test takes 30-60 minutes; mild discomfort is normal but pain is brief.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy with intraepidermal nerve fibre density (IENFD)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard test for small-fibre neuropathy when nerve conduction studies are normal but symptoms suggest distal painful neuropathy. Quantifies unmyelinated nerve endings per millimetre of epidermis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3 mm punch biopsy is taken from the distal leg and sometimes the thigh under local anaesthetic. The wound heals in 1-2 weeks; sutures are usually not required.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the brain, spinal cord, or affected plexus
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural lesions producing the pain — disc herniation with nerve root compression, spinal cord lesion, brain infarct or demyelinating plaque, tumour involving a nerve, or vascular conflict in trigeminal neuralgia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lying still in a scanner for 20-45 minutes. Contrast is used selectively. Earplugs reduce noise; claustrophobia can be managed with mild sedation if needed.

diagnosticTests.test: HbA1c, fasting glucose, B12, folate, thyroid function, renal and liver panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the most common reversible or modifiable causes of distal symmetric polyneuropathy — diabetes, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, uraemia, and hepatic disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with results in 1-3 days in most labs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised measurement of thermal, vibration, and mechanical detection and pain thresholds. Used to phenotype patients for treatment selection and research.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute session with calibrated probes that deliver controlled stimuli; the patient signals when they first detect a sensation or when it becomes painful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A targeted local-anaesthetic injection around a suspected nerve, plexus, or facet joint to confirm the pain generator before considering a longer-term intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An image-guided injection (ultrasound or fluoroscopy) lasting 10-20 minutes. Brief soreness at the site is common; relief — if the right target — typically begins within minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of neuropathic pain follows the 2015 NeuPSIG recommendations (Finnerup, Lancet Neurology), the 2019 NICE NG193 guideline, and the 2022 American Academy of Neurology guideline on painful diabetic neuropathy. First-line pharmacotherapy is one of four drug classes: gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, or a tricyclic antidepressant such as amitriptyline or nortriptyline. Each has an FDA-approved or guideline-supported indication for at least one neuropathic pain condition, and head-to-head trials show broadly similar efficacy with a number needed to treat (NNT) of roughly 4-7 for 50% pain reduction. Choice between agents is driven by comorbidity, side-effect profile, and access — duloxetine for coexisting depression or anxiety, amitriptyline for nighttime sleep, gabapentin and pregabalin for renal-dose-adjusted use and rapid onset, and topical agents for localised pain. The 5% lidocaine patch and 8% capsaicin patch (QUTENZA) are first-line in postherpetic neuralgia and other localised peripheral neuropathic pain. Opioids and tramadol are third-line because of modest efficacy, tolerance, and harm at population scale; current guidelines explicitly discourage routine opioid initiation. Tapentadol and tramadol have some neuropathic activity through noradrenergic effects but should be reserved for refractory cases. For patients who fail two or more first-line agents at adequate dose and duration, interventional options include image-guided steroid injections for radicular pain, peripheral nerve blocks, dorsal root ganglion (DRG) stimulation for focal pain in CRPS and post-surgical neuropathy, and 10 kHz high-frequency spinal cord stimulation, which produced over 70% sustained responder rates at 24 months in the SENZA-RCT and SENZA-PDN trials for back-and-leg pain and painful diabetic neuropathy respectively. Treating the underlying cause — glycaemic control, removal of the offending drug, decompression of a compressed root, or immunomodulation in autoimmune neuropathy — is essential and runs in parallel. Non-pharmacological care is part of every plan: cognitive-behavioural therapy, graded exercise, sleep optimisation, and structured patient education improve pain interference and quality of life even when pain intensity is only modestly reduced.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (start 100-300 mg at night; titrate to 900-3600 mg/day divided in 3 doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Binds the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release in the dorsal horn. FDA-approved for postherpetic neuralgia; widely used off-label for painful diabetic neuropathy and other peripheral neuropathic pain. Renal dose adjustment required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT for 50% pain reduction approximately 6-8 across neuropathic pain conditions (Wiffen Cochrane 2017).

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin (start 75 mg twice daily; titrate to 150-600 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Same alpha-2-delta mechanism as gabapentin with more predictable absorption and faster titration. FDA-approved for painful diabetic neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia, spinal cord injury pain, and fibromyalgia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT approximately 4-7 for 50% pain reduction; about 30% of patients reach this endpoint in pivotal DPN trials (Lesser 2004, Rosenstock 2004).

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (start 30 mg once daily; titrate to 60-120 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) that enhances descending inhibitory pain control. FDA-approved for painful diabetic neuropathy, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. Particularly useful when depression or anxiety coexists.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT approximately 5-6 for 50% pain reduction in DPN (Lunn Cochrane 2014); positive AAN 2022 recommendation.

medicalTreatments.name: Amitriptyline or nortriptyline (start 10-25 mg at bedtime; titrate to 25-75 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Tricyclic antidepressants with serotonergic, noradrenergic, anticholinergic, and sodium-channel-blocking effects. Effective across most neuropathic pain conditions. Nortriptyline is better tolerated than amitriptyline in older adults. ECG screening recommended above 100 mg/day or with cardiac history.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT approximately 3.6 for 50% pain reduction across neuropathic pain (Moore Cochrane 2015); among the most efficacious agents but limited by side effects.

medicalTreatments.name: 5% lidocaine patch (apply 1-3 patches over painful area for 12 hours on, 12 hours off)
medicalTreatments.description: Sodium-channel-blocking topical patch that produces analgesia without significant systemic absorption. First-line for localised peripheral neuropathic pain including postherpetic neuralgia in elderly patients and patients with multiple comorbidities.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: About 50% of postherpetic neuralgia patients achieve at least 30% pain reduction (Galer 2002); excellent safety profile.

medicalTreatments.name: 8% capsaicin patch (QUTENZA, single 30-60 minute application repeated every 12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: High-concentration topical capsaicin defunctionalises TRPV1-expressing nociceptors. FDA-approved for postherpetic neuralgia and painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy of the feet (STEP study).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: About 40-45% of PHN patients achieve 30% pain reduction at week 12 (Backonja 2008); single application provides 12 weeks of analgesia in responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Tramadol (50-100 mg every 4-6 hours; maximum 400 mg/day) and tapentadol
medicalTreatments.description: Opioid agonists with additional monoaminergic activity. Second- or third-line for neuropathic pain when first-line agents fail or are contraindicated. Risk of dependence, serotonin syndrome with SSRIs/SNRIs, and seizures.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT approximately 4.7 for 50% pain reduction (Duehmke Cochrane 2017); useful as bridging therapy but not preferred long-term.

medicalTreatments.name: Carbamazepine (200-1200 mg/day divided) or oxcarbazepine (300-1800 mg/day divided)
medicalTreatments.description: Sodium-channel-blocking antiepileptics. Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia and produces dramatic, near-complete pain relief in most patients. Requires monitoring for hyponatraemia, blood dyscrasias, and HLA-B*1502 in patients of Asian ancestry.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Over 70% initial response in trigeminal neuralgia (Wiffen Cochrane 2014); benefit attenuates over years and many patients eventually need surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Epidural or transforaminal corticosteroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Image-guided injection of steroid plus local anaesthetic around an inflamed nerve root in radicular pain. Useful for short- and medium-term relief, especially as a bridge to recovery or to defer surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50-70% of patients with acute lumbar radicular pain achieve clinically meaningful relief at 1-3 months; effect attenuates beyond 6 months (NICE NG193 review).

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS), including 10 kHz high-frequency systems
surgicalOptions.description: Implantable epidural electrode array that modulates dorsal horn signalling. Reserved for failed-back-surgery-syndrome, painful diabetic neuropathy refractory to medication, and CRPS. SENZA-RCT (Kapural 2015) showed 10 kHz SCS superior to traditional tonic stimulation for back and leg pain at 24 months; SENZA-PDN extended responder benefit to painful diabetic neuropathy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Roughly 60-70% sustained responders (≥50% pain relief) at 12-24 months in current high-frequency systems.

surgicalOptions.name: Dorsal root ganglion (DRG) stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: Targets the DRG directly using small electrode leads. ACCURATE trial demonstrated superiority of DRG over conventional SCS for lower-limb complex regional pain syndrome and post-surgical neuropathy at 3 months and 12 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Roughly 75% responders at 3 months and 65% at 12 months in CRPS-I/causalgia (Deer 2017).

surgicalOptions.name: Microvascular decompression for trigeminal neuralgia
surgicalOptions.description: Open posterior fossa surgery that mobilises a compressing vessel off the trigeminal nerve. Considered when carbamazepine and other medical therapies fail or produce intolerable side effects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain-free outcome in 80-90%; durable freedom at 10 years in roughly 70%.

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive spine surgery (microdiscectomy, laminectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Removes compressive disc material, bone, or ligament producing radicular pain in selected patients with concordant imaging and persistent neurological deficit or severe pain.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Roughly 75-80% achieve significant leg-pain relief at 1-2 years; back pain improves less reliably (SPORT trial).

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Improves pain interference, mood, sleep, and function with moderate effect sizes in randomised trials and Cochrane reviews. Often delivered alongside pharmacotherapy in pain-management programmes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-risk option with modest evidence for short-term relief in diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Reasonable to trial; not a substitute for first-line pharmacotherapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Alpha-lipoic acid (600 mg IV or oral daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Antioxidant with positive trials in symptomatic diabetic neuropathy (NATHAN-1, SYDNEY trials). Modest effect; widely used in Germany and selected European centres.

whySeeSpecialist: Pain medicine specialist referral is warranted when two or more first-line agents at adequate dose and duration have failed, when an interventional option such as a nerve block or spinal cord stimulator is being considered, when pain involves a complex distribution (CRPS, central pain), or when high-risk medication combinations (opioids, multiple sedating agents) are being layered. A neurologist should be involved when the diagnosis is uncertain, when central nervous system disease is suspected, or when small-fibre neuropathy needs skin biopsy confirmation. Spine surgery referral is appropriate for radicular pain with progressive neurological deficit or imaging-concordant compressive lesions.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for fellowship training in pain medicine, board certification, and access to image-guided injection, neuromodulation, and a structured multidisciplinary pain programme that includes a clinical psychologist and physical therapist. Ask whether the clinic uses validated outcome measures (Brief Pain Inventory, neuropathic pain scale, PROMIS) and follows current guidelines rather than defaulting to opioid prescribing. Continuity matters — neuropathic pain is a long-term condition managed over years, and abrupt provider changes degrade outcomes.
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate adults over 50 with recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) — over 90% protection against shingles and approximately 88% against postherpetic neuralgia in the ZOE-50 and ZOE-70 trials
Maintain HbA1c below 7.0% in type 1 diabetes and an individualised target (typically 6.5-7.5%) in type 2 diabetes to slow progression of diabetic neuropathy
Replete vitamin B12 in patients on long-term metformin or proton-pump inhibitors and screen older adults — replacement reverses early neuropathy

Use the lowest effective dose of neurotoxic chemotherapy and consider dose reduction or substitution at the first sign of neuropathy; duloxetine has prevention-of-progression data for established CIPN
Treat herpes zoster within 72 hours with antiviral therapy (valaciclovir, famciclovir, aciclovir) to shorten the acute attack and modestly reduce PHN risk
Limit alcohol intake to under 14 standard drinks per week and avoid prolonged heavy drinking that produces nutritional neuropathy
lifestyleModifications: Sleep optimisation — set consistent sleep and wake times, treat coexisting insomnia and obstructive sleep apnoea, and time sedating medications to night to support 7-8 hours of sleep
Graded aerobic and resistance exercise — 150 minutes weekly of moderate-intensity activity reduces neuropathic pain intensity and improves nerve fibre density in diabetic neuropathy (Kluding 2012)
Smoking cessation — nicotine accelerates microvascular nerve damage and reduces analgesic response
Foot care for patients with diabetic neuropathy — daily inspection, well-fitted footwear, podiatry review, and prompt treatment of any wound to prevent ulceration
Structured pain self-management programme — pacing, activity planning, and cognitive reframing reduce pain interference even when intensity changes little
Address mood and anxiety actively — untreated depression and anxiety amplify central sensitisation and reduce response to every analgesic class
Limit alcohol to under 7 drinks per week if there is established neuropathy and avoid binge drinking entirely
Maintain a 2-week pain and trigger diary at the start of treatment to identify personal flare patterns and to monitor response objectively

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating pattern rich in vegetables, fruit, legumes, fish, and olive oil — reduces inflammation and improves nerve health in observational studies
Oily fish twice weekly (salmon, sardines, mackerel) for omega-3 fatty acids that support myelin and nerve membrane health
Whole grains, legumes, and nuts as primary carbohydrate sources to support stable blood glucose in diabetes-related neuropathy
B-vitamin-rich foods (leafy greens, eggs, fortified cereals, legumes) and lean protein for nerve repair
Adequate hydration — at least 2 litres daily unless restricted for cardiac or renal reasons
avoid: Ultra-processed foods and sugar-sweetened beverages that drive postprandial glucose excursions in people with diabetes
Excess alcohol — more than 7 drinks per week worsens established neuropathy and impairs nutrient absorption
Restrictive crash diets without medical supervision — can worsen B12, B1, and copper status
Foods that trigger known migraine or trigeminal neuralgia attacks in susceptible patients (individualised; common triggers include alcohol, aged cheese, and processed meats)
Energy drinks and high-caffeine intake late in the day if they worsen sleep, which independently amplifies pain

exerciseGuidelines: Graded aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) for 30 minutes most days plus 2 sessions of resistance training weekly improves neuropathic pain intensity, sleep, mood, and small-fibre nerve density in diabetic neuropathy. Begin at a level you can sustain without a 24-hour pain flare and progress by no more than 10% per week. For patients with significant loss of protective sensation in the feet, pool-based exercise and stationary cycling reduce ulceration risk. Stop and reassess any activity that produces sharp shooting pain, new weakness, or unsteady gait.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed medications at consistent times — gabapentinoids and tricyclics need steady levels to maintain analgesia and reduce side effects
Apply topical agents (lidocaine 5% or capsaicin) at the same time each day and rotate sites if redness develops
Inspect feet daily and after any walking session if you have diabetic neuropathy with loss of protective sensation
Track pain on a 0-10 scale and note sleep, mood, and activity in a brief weekly log so the treating clinician can adjust therapy on data, not memory
Use pacing for high-pain days — alternate 20-30 minute activity blocks with 10-15 minute rest blocks instead of pushing to exhaustion
Plan medication refills 7-10 days early; abrupt discontinuation of gabapentinoids, tricyclics, or duloxetine can produce withdrawal symptoms
prognosis: Long-term outlook depends heavily on the underlying cause and how quickly effective treatment begins. Postherpetic neuralgia improves in roughly 50% of patients within 12 months even without intervention, with a smaller fraction having persistent pain at 5 years. Diabetic peripheral neuropathy progresses slowly when glycaemic control is poor and stabilises in many patients with tight control plus first-line pharmacotherapy. Radicular pain from disc herniation resolves in roughly 70% of patients within 6-12 weeks of conservative care. Central post-stroke pain and chronic post-surgical neuropathic pain are more persistent — only about 30-40% achieve substantial long-term relief on pharmacotherapy alone, and many require neuromodulation. Across all causes, around 30% of patients reach a 50% pain reduction on first-line monotherapy, another 20-30% improve with combination therapy, and the remainder need interventional or multidisciplinary pain programme support. Even when pain intensity is only modestly reduced, structured care improves function, sleep, mood, and quality of life — the outcomes patients value most. The single strongest predictor of a poor trajectory is delay of more than 6 months between symptom onset and the start of evidence-based treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Pharmacotherapy effects take time to assess. Gabapentin and pregabalin show benefit within 1-2 weeks of reaching an adequate dose. Duloxetine and tricyclic antidepressants typically need 4-6 weeks at target dose for full analgesic effect. Topical 5% lidocaine produces relief within hours but plateaus over days. A single 8% capsaicin application produces stable analgesia from week 2 through week 12 in responders. Spinal cord stimulator trials are usually 5-7 days; permanent implants show stable response by 3 months. Following recommended care, expect the first meaningful improvement at 2-6 weeks and a realistic stable endpoint at 3-6 months; treatment then continues to maintain that response.
complications: Sleep deprivation and depression — present in 30-50% of patients with persistent neuropathic pain and themselves worsen pain perception
Opioid dependence when opioids are prescribed long-term without clear benefit; current guidelines explicitly recommend against routine opioid initiation
Falls and fractures in older adults with peripheral neuropathy and impaired proprioception, especially when combined with sedating medication
Diabetic foot ulceration, infection, and amputation when sensory neuropathy is unrecognised — annual foot examination prevents most events
Suicidal ideation — neuropathic pain is associated with a 2-3 fold increase in suicidal thoughts; routine screening is recommended in chronic pain care
Cardiovascular and metabolic side effects of tricyclic antidepressants in older adults, including orthostatic hypotension, urinary retention, and QT prolongation
Withdrawal syndromes from abrupt discontinuation of gabapentinoids, SNRIs, or tricyclics — always taper rather than stop

supportResources.name: International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP) — Neuropathic Pain Resources
supportResources.description: Definitions, grading algorithms, and patient-facing resources from the global authority on pain.

supportResources.name: American Chronic Pain Association
supportResources.description: US patient organisation with self-management tools, peer support, and educational material on neuropathic pain conditions.

supportResources.name: Foundation for Peripheral Neuropathy
supportResources.description: US patient organisation focused on peripheral neuropathy with patient guides, research updates, and support networks.

supportResources.name: NICE NG193 — Chronic Pain in Over 16s
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline on assessment and management of chronic primary pain and chronic secondary pain, freely accessible.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia produces widespread pain with tender points, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms but lacks a single demonstrable nerve lesion. Neuropathic pain follows a neuroanatomical territory and has objective sensory signs; fibromyalgia pain is diffuse and is classified as nociplastic. Both can coexist and respond partially to duloxetine and pregabalin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Carpal tunnel syndrome is a focal median mononeuropathy at the wrist with classic nocturnal numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. It is a neuropathic pain condition but localised and typically curable by surgical decompression — distinct from generalised peripheral neuropathic pain that requires systemic therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CRPS produces severe pain plus autonomic, motor, and trophic changes in a limb after injury, often without a clearly demonstrable nerve lesion (CRPS-I) or with one (CRPS-II). Unlike length-dependent neuropathy, CRPS is regional rather than territorial and is graded by the Budapest criteria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sciatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sciatica is one specific neuropathic pain syndrome — radicular pain in the L4-S1 distribution from nerve root compression — rather than a separate disease. It is mentioned here because patients often use the term to mean any leg pain; a careful examination distinguishes radicular sciatica from referred lumbar facet pain or hip pathology.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying cause of painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy, the most common form of neuropathic pain worldwide.

relatedConditions.name: Postherpetic Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Specific neuropathic pain syndrome after shingles; vaccinatable risk factor and a model condition for first-line therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Distinct facial neuropathic pain syndrome with a specialised first-line drug (carbamazepine) and surgical options.

relatedConditions.name: Sciatica
relatedConditions.relevance: Radicular neuropathic pain from lumbar nerve root compression; the most common cause of leg-distributed neuropathic pain.

relatedConditions.name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS)
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe regional neuropathic pain after limb injury; shares interventional treatment pathways such as DRG and spinal cord stimulation.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Central nervous system demyelinating disease that produces central neuropathic pain, including trigeminal neuralgia and Lhermitte sign, in a substantial minority of patients.

faqs.question: What does neuropathic pain feel like?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe neuropathic pain as burning, electric-shock-like, shooting, or stabbing, often with tingling, pins-and-needles, and numbness in the same area. Light touch, clothing, or a breeze can feel painful (allodynia), and the skin may feel oddly numb and intensely painful at the same time. Pain is often worst at night and disturbs sleep.

faqs.question: How is neuropathic pain different from regular pain?
faqs.answer: Regular (nociceptive) pain is the nervous system correctly reporting tissue injury such as a cut, burn, or arthritic joint. Neuropathic pain is the nervous system itself misfiring after damage to a nerve, the spinal cord, or the brain. It does not respond well to standard painkillers like paracetamol, NSAIDs, or opioids and needs different first-line drugs.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of neuropathic pain?
faqs.answer: The leading causes are diabetes (painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy), shingles (postherpetic neuralgia), nerve root compression from the spine (sciatica and cervical radiculopathy), chemotherapy, stroke (central post-stroke pain), spinal cord injury, surgery and trauma, and trigeminal neuralgia. Less common causes include autoimmune neuropathy, HIV, heavy alcohol use, and vitamin B12 deficiency.

faqs.question: How is neuropathic pain diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is mainly clinical. A clinician maps the pain to a known nerve territory, looks for a relevant injury or disease in the history, and examines the area for altered sensation. Validated screening questionnaires (painDETECT, DN4, LANSS) help in primary care. Nerve conduction studies, skin biopsy for small-fibre density, and MRI are added when needed to confirm and localise the lesion.

faqs.question: What is the best first-line treatment for neuropathic pain?
faqs.answer: Current guidelines (NeuPSIG 2015, NICE NG193, AAN 2022) recommend starting with one of four agents: gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, or a tricyclic antidepressant such as amitriptyline or nortriptyline. Topical 5% lidocaine and 8% capsaicin patches are first-line for localised pain such as postherpetic neuralgia. Choice depends on other conditions, side effects, and access.

faqs.question: Do opioids work for neuropathic pain?
faqs.answer: Opioids have modest short-term efficacy in neuropathic pain but tolerance, dependence, and harms grow with long-term use. Major guidelines (NeuPSIG, NICE, CDC) place opioids as third-line at best and recommend against routine initiation. Tramadol and tapentadol, which also act on noradrenergic pathways, may be used in selected refractory cases under specialist care.

faqs.question: How long do gabapentin and pregabalin take to work?
faqs.answer: Both drugs need titration. Initial relief can appear within 1-2 weeks of reaching an adequate dose, with full effect by 4-6 weeks. Doses are increased gradually to limit dizziness, drowsiness, and swelling. About 30% of patients reach 50% pain reduction; if there is no meaningful response at maximum tolerated dose by week 6-8, switch to a different drug class rather than stacking similar agents.

faqs.question: Can neuropathic pain go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Some forms resolve. Roughly half of postherpetic neuralgia cases improve within a year, and most acute radicular pain from disc herniation settles within 6-12 weeks. Diabetic, central post-stroke, and post-surgical neuropathic pain tend to persist and need ongoing treatment. Early evidence-based care improves the chance of recovery and prevents central sensitisation that makes pain harder to treat later.

faqs.question: Is neuropathic pain curable?
faqs.answer: Some causes are curable when treated early — vitamin B12 replacement, alcohol cessation, decompression of a compressed nerve root, and treatment of an underlying autoimmune disease can resolve the pain. Many other forms are not curable but are controllable: most patients achieve meaningful pain reduction with first-line drugs, topical agents, or interventional therapy, allowing better sleep, mood, and function.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause nerve pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy affects roughly 20-25% of people with diabetes for 10 years or more. It usually begins as burning and tingling in the toes and spreads upward in a stocking pattern. Tight glycaemic control slows progression, and duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin, and topical capsaicin are first-line treatments per the 2022 AAN guideline.

faqs.question: What is postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Postherpetic neuralgia is persistent dermatomal pain lasting more than 3 months after a shingles rash. It is caused by varicella-zoster damage to sensory neurons and affects 10-15% of patients over 60 who develop shingles. First-line treatments include the 5% lidocaine patch, 8% capsaicin patch, gabapentin, pregabalin, and tricyclic antidepressants.

faqs.question: Can the shingles vaccine prevent nerve pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. The recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) prevents over 90% of shingles cases and approximately 88% of postherpetic neuralgia in adults over 50 in the ZOE-50 and ZOE-70 trials. Two doses are given 2-6 months apart. It is the most effective single intervention to prevent one of the most common causes of long-term neuropathic pain.

faqs.question: Does chemotherapy cause permanent nerve pain?
faqs.answer: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy affects 30-40% of patients on platinum agents, taxanes, vinca alkaloids, or bortezomib. Symptoms often improve over months after treatment ends, but roughly 30% have persistent symptoms at 6 months and a smaller share have long-term pain. Duloxetine has the strongest evidence for established CIPN; dose modification during treatment reduces risk.

faqs.question: When should I see a pain specialist?
faqs.answer: Ask for a pain medicine referral when two or more first-line drugs at adequate dose and duration have failed, when interventional treatment such as a nerve block or spinal cord stimulator is being considered, when opioid use is escalating, or when pain involves a complex distribution such as CRPS or central pain. A neurologist should be involved when the diagnosis or underlying disease is uncertain.

faqs.question: What is spinal cord stimulation and how well does it work?
faqs.answer: Spinal cord stimulation uses a small implanted electrode array in the epidural space to modulate dorsal horn signalling. In the SENZA-RCT, 10 kHz high-frequency stimulation produced sustained responder rates above 70% at 24 months for back and leg pain. The SENZA-PDN trial extended benefit to painful diabetic neuropathy. A 5-7 day external trial is used to confirm response before permanent implantation.

faqs.question: Can exercise help neuropathic pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Structured aerobic and resistance exercise reduces pain intensity, improves sleep and mood, and in diabetic neuropathy increases small-fibre nerve density in the skin (Kluding 2012). Aim for 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week, building gradually. Pool-based exercise and stationary cycling are good options for patients with foot ulcer risk.

faqs.question: Is cannabis effective for nerve pain?
faqs.answer: Cannabinoids show a small to modest analgesic effect in neuropathic pain in randomised trials and Cochrane reviews, with notable side effects including dizziness, sedation, and cognitive effects. Major guidelines list cannabinoids as fourth-line or experimental, behind first-line drugs, topical agents, and interventional options. Regulation and product quality vary widely by country.

faqs.question: Can stress and anxiety make nerve pain worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anxiety, depression, and poor sleep amplify central sensitisation and reduce response to every analgesic class. Cognitive behavioural therapy, acceptance and commitment therapy, and treatment of coexisting depression improve pain interference and function even when pain intensity is only modestly reduced. Mental health care is part of a complete plan, not an add-on.

faqs.question: How is trigeminal neuralgia treated?
faqs.answer: First-line drug therapy is carbamazepine, with over 70% initial response, or oxcarbazepine when tolerability matters. Patients with Asian ancestry should be screened for HLA-B*1502 before carbamazepine due to severe skin reaction risk. When medications fail or side effects are intolerable, microvascular decompression, gamma knife radiosurgery, and percutaneous procedures are effective surgical options.

faqs.question: Are topical patches worth trying?
faqs.answer: Yes for localised peripheral neuropathic pain. The 5% lidocaine patch is well-tolerated and first-line in postherpetic neuralgia, particularly in older adults. The 8% capsaicin patch (QUTENZA) is applied once in a clinic and provides up to 12 weeks of analgesia in responders. Both avoid systemic side effects and combine well with oral therapy.

faqs.question: Can children get neuropathic pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, though less commonly than adults. Causes include traumatic nerve injury, chemotherapy, inherited sodium-channel disorders, CRPS, and post-surgical pain. Treatment principles are similar but doses are weight-based and the evidence base is smaller. Pediatric pain specialists and multidisciplinary programmes give the best outcomes.

metaTitle: Neuropathic Pain: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Neuropathic pain: burning or shooting nerve-damage pain from diabetes, shingles, sciatica, or stroke. Treatment reviewed by our medical board.
